_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to use mathematical modelling with linear relations to solve applied problems.

_source.w: We are learning to use mathematical modelling with linear relations to solve applied problems.
y: Mathematical modelling with linear relations allows us to predict, compare and optimise in practical situations.
The modelling cycle — formulate, solve, interpret, evaluate — develops our ability to think critically about real problems.
Linear models are used in business (cost analysis), science (motion) and engineering (structural analysis).
eg: Modelling the cost of a phone plan: C = 0.10n + 20, where n is the number of calls.
Comparing two pricing models graphically and finding the break-even point.
Setting up a linear model for distance-time: 'A car travels at 80 km/h. Model distance as a function of time.'
Using a linear model to predict future values: 'If temperature increases by 2°C per hour, what will it be at 3 pm?'
Evaluating whether a linear model is appropriate for a given dataset.
Interpreting the gradient of a model: 'The gradient of 0.10 means each call costs 10 cents.'
